pages.title: Relations between global and local results. Affine problems related to duality
pages.html:
1 Relations between global and local results. Affine problems related to duality

It is well known that many statements concerning a projective scheme \(X\) can be formulated in terms of statements concerning a certain graded ring, or better a complete local ring, namely the homogeneous coordinate ring of \(X\) (i.e. the affine ring of the projecting cone \(\widetilde{X}\) of \(X\)), or its completion (i.e. the completion of the local ring of the vertex of \(\widetilde{X}\)). The interest of this reformulation is that it often allows, starting from known global results, to conjecture, or even to prove, analogous results for complete noetherian local rings more general than those which actually occur in the global statement, for example for local rings which are not necessarily of equal characteristic. Thus, Serre's duality theorem for projective space XII 1.1 suggested the useful local duality theorem V.5.6. Serre's fundamental theorem on the cohomology of coherent modules on projective space (finiteness, asymptotic behaviour for large \(n\) of \(\operatorname{H}^i(X, F(n))\), cf. EGA III 2.2.1) generalizes to a structure theorem for the local invariants \(\operatorname{H}^i_{\mathfrak{m}}(M)\), see V.5.7. Likewise, the Lefschetz theorems for the fundamental group and the Picard group (" equivalence criteria"), well familiar in the classical case and subsequently extended to an arbitrary base field, suggested the "local" Lefschetz theorems of the exposés X and XI. Of course, the local theorems in their turn are valuable tools for obtaining global statements. For example local duality allows one to formulate a global asymptotic property XII 1.3 (i) by the vanishing of certain local invariants \(\operatorname{H}^i(F_x)\). More substantially, the local Lefschetz theorems, implying for example the "purity" or the parafactoriality of certain complete intersection local rings (X 3.4 and XI 3.13) make it possible, in the global Lefschetz theorems, to dispense with certain hypotheses of nonsingularity, as in X 3.5, 3.6, 3.7.

Another useful generalization of the theorems concerning projective schemes over a field \(k\) consists in replacing \(k\) by a general base scheme. Thus, the continuation of EGA III will give a generalization in this sense of Serre duality1; the finiteness theorems and those on the asymptotic behaviour of the \(\operatorname{H}^i(X, F(n))\) have been stated in EGA III 2.2.1 over a general base scheme, and finally the Lefschetz theorems can also be developed for a projective morphism, as was seen in XII 4.9, thanks to the local theorem XII 4.7. Of course, working over a general base scheme also leads to essentially new statements, such as the "comparison theorem" EGA III 4.15 and the existence theorem for sheaves EGA III 5.1.4 (which, as has moreover been seen in IX, stem from the same cohomological key theorems as the Lefschetz theorems for \(\pi_1\) and \(\operatorname{Pic}\)).

It is then necessary to bring out theorems that simultaneously encompass the two generalizations we have just pointed out of the statements concerning projective schemes over a field. The natural objects for such a common generalization are the noetherian rings separated and complete for an \(I\)-adic topology. Their study, from this point of view, has not yet been seriously undertaken, and seems to me at present the most interesting subject in the local theory of coherent sheaves. Here is a typical problem in this direction:

Conjecture 1.1

["Second affine finiteness theorem"\(^{(**)}\)]

Let \(M\) be a module of finite type over a noetherian ring \(A\) (which will be assumed if necessary to be a quotient of a regular ring), \(J\) an ideal of \(A\); prove that the modules \(\operatorname{H}^i_J(M)\) are "\(J\)-cofinite", i.e. that the modules

\[ \operatorname{Hom}_A(A/J, \operatorname{H}^i_J(M)) \]

are of finite type.

Let us recall that one denotes by \(\operatorname{H}^i_J (M)\) the module \(\operatorname{H}^i_Y (X, \widetilde{M})\) (where \(X=\operatorname{Spec} (A)\), \(Y=V(J)\)) of Exp. I, interpreted in II in terms of an inductive limit of cohomologies of Koszul complexes, or again for \(i\geqslant 2\) the module \(\operatorname{H}^{i-1} (X-Y, \widetilde{M})\). To tell the truth, 1.1 ought to be a consequence of a more precise statement, implying that the \(\operatorname{H}^i_J ({M})\) lie in a suitable abelian subcategory \({\underline{D}_J}\) of the category \({\underline{C}_J}\) of \(A\)-modules with support \(\subset Y=V(J)\), such that \(H\in \operatorname{Ob} {\underline{D}_J}\) implies that \(H\) is \(J\)-cofinite. (NB Unfortunately the category of modules \(H\) with support contained in \(V(J)\) and which are \(J\)-cofinite is not stable under passage to the quotient!). The essential problem would then consist in defining \({\underline{D}_J}\). More precisely, the solution of problem 1.1 ought to come (at least if \(A\) is a quotient of a regular ring) from a duality theory, generalizing at once local duality and the duality theory of projective morphisms alluded to above, and which would be of the following kind:

Conjecture 1.2

["Affine duality"] Suppose \(A\) regular, separated and complete for the \(J\)-adic topology. Let \(C^{\bullet}(A)\) be an injective resolution of \(A\).

  1. Prove that the functor

    \[ D_J: L_{\bullet}\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \operatorname{Hom}_J(L_{\bullet}, C^{\bullet}(A)) \]

    from the category of complexes of \(A\)-modules, free of finite type in every dimension and bounded above in degree, (where the morphisms are the homomorphisms of complexes up to homotopy) to the category of complexes of \(A\)-modules \(K^{\bullet}\), injective in every dimension and bounded above in degree (where the homomorphisms are defined in the same way), is fully faithful.

  2. Prove that for every \(K^{\bullet}\) of the form \(D_J(L_{\bullet})\), the \(\operatorname{H}^i(K^{\bullet})(= \operatorname{Ext}_Y^i (X; L_{\bullet}, \mathscr{O}_X))\) are \(J\)-cofinite.

  3. More precisely, prove that the \(K^{\bullet}\) which are homotopic to a complex of the form \(D_J(L_{\bullet})\) can be characterized by finiteness properties of the \(\operatorname{H}^i(K^{\bullet})\), stronger than the one considered in (ii), for example by the property \(\operatorname{H}^i(K^{\bullet})\in \operatorname{Ob} {\underline{D}_J}\), where \({\underline{D}_J}\) is a suitable abelian category, as considered above.

Let us note that the problem is resolved in the affirmative when \(A\) is local and \(J\) is an ideal of definition of it (cf. Exp. IV), and also when \(J\) is the zero ideal. In these two cases, exceptionally, one can confine oneself to taking for \({\underline{D}_J}\) the category of modules with support \(V(J)\) which are \(J\)-cofinite, (which in the second case simply means that one takes the category of modules of finite type over \(A\)). An affirmative solution of conjecture 1.2 in general would yield one for 1.1, by taking for \(L_{\bullet}\) the dual of a free resolution of finite type of \(M\). On the other hand, an affirmative solution of 1.1 would give an affirmative answer to the first part of the following conjecture, which we formulate in "global" form:

Conjecture 1.3

Let \(X\subset \mathbf{P}^r_k\) be a closed subscheme of the standard projective scheme which is locally a complete intersection and every irreducible component of which is of codimension \(\geqslant s\). Let \(U=\mathbf{P}^r_k -X\).

  1. Prove that for every coherent Module \(F\) on \(U\), one has 2

    \[ \dim \operatorname{H}^i(U, F)<+\infty{\rm \ for\ } i\geqslant s.{} \]

  2. Give an example, with \(X\) connected and regular, where one has

    \[ \operatorname{H}^s(U, F)\neq 0. \]

To see that (i) is a particular case of 1.1 one considers

\[ \operatorname{H}^i(U, F(\cdot))=\coprod_n \operatorname{H}^i (U, F(n))= \operatorname{H}^i(E^{r+1} -X, \widetilde{F}) \]

as a module over the affine ring \(k[t_0, \ldots, t_r]\) of the projecting cone \(E^{r+1}\) of \(\mathbf{P}^r\). This module is none other than \(\operatorname{H}_J^{i+1}(M)\), where \(J\) is the ideal of the projecting cone \(\widetilde{X}\) of \(X\) in \(E^{r+1}\). On the other hand, from the hypothesis made on \(X\), which implies that \(X\) is also a complete intersection of codimension \(\geqslant s\) at every point of \(E^{r+1}\) distinct from the origin, it follows that \(\operatorname{H}^{i+1}_J(M)\) is zero outside the origin for \(i\geqslant s\). If it is therefore \(J\)-cofinite as 1.1 would have it, it is a fortiori \(\mathfrak{m}\)-cofinite, which easily implies that it is of finite dimension in every degree. Note moreover that conjecture 1.3 already arises for a nonsingular irreducible curve \(X\) in \(\mathbf{P}^3\); it is not known whether in this case the \(\operatorname{H}^2(\mathbf{P}^3-X, \mathscr{O}_X(n))\) are of finite dimension, or whether they are necessarily zero3. It is not even known whether there exists an irreducible curve of \(\mathbf{P}^3\) which is not set-theoretically the intersection of two hypersurfaces.

Problem 1.4

Give an affine variant of the "comparison theorem" EGA III 4.1.5 as a theorem on the commutation of the functors \(\operatorname{H}^i_J\) with certain projective limits.

Finally, in the present order of ideas, I had posed the following problem: Let \(A\) be a regular noetherian local ring complete, \(K\) its field of fractions, prove that \(\operatorname{Ext}^i_A(K, A)=0\) for all \(i\). An affirmative answer was given on the spot by Mr AUSLANDER; the regularity hypothesis can be replaced by the assumption that \(A\) is integral, and it is in fact true that \(\operatorname{Ext}^i_A(K, M)=0\) for all \(i\), as soon as \(M\) is of finite type over \(A\). This follows immediately from the following statement, due to AUSLANDER: if \(A\) is a complete noetherian local ring, then for every module of finite type \(M\) over \(A\), the functors \(\operatorname{Ext}_A^i(., M)\) transform inductive limits into projective limits.

pages.title: Problems related to \(\pi_0\): local Bertini theorems
pages.html:
2 Problems related to \(\pi_0\): local Bertini theorems

Let \(A\) be a complete noetherian local ring, \(f\) an element of its maximal ideal, \(X=\operatorname{Spec} (A)\), \(Y=\operatorname{Spec}(A/fA)\). The use of the local "Lefschetz" technique makes it possible to give criteria for \(Y'=X'\cap Y\) (where \(X'=X-\{\mathfrak{m}\}\)) to be connected, in terms of hypotheses on \(X'\). Thus, it suffices that one have: a) \(X'\) connected b) \(\operatorname{prof} \mathscr{O}_{X', x} \geqslant 2\) for every closed point \(x\) of \(X'\) c) \(f\) is \(A\)-regular. One will note however that hypotheses b) and c) are not of a purely topological nature, for example are not invariant when replacing \(X\) by \(X_{\text{red}}\). In the analogous situation for a projective scheme \(X'\) over a field and a hyperplane section \(Y'\) of \(X'\), the use of the "Bertini theorem" and of Zariski's "connectedness theorem" makes it possible in fact to obtain results of a distinctly more satisfactory appearance, which had led me in the oral seminar to state a conjecture, which I have since resolved in the affirmative. Let us therefore state here:

Theorem 2.1

Let \(A\) be a complete noetherian local ring, \(X\) its spectrum, \(\mathfrak{a}\) the closed point of \(X\), \(X'=X-\{ \mathfrak{a}\}\). Suppose that \(X\) satisfies the conditions (where \(k\) denotes an integer \(\geqslant 1\)):

  • a\(_k\))

    The irreducible components of \(X'\) are of dimension \(\geqslant k+1\).

  • b\(_k\))

    \(X'\) is connected in dimension \(\geqslant k\), i.e. one cannot disconnect \(X'\) by a closed subset of dimension \(<k\) (cf. III 3.8).

Let \(m\) be an integer, \(0\leqslant m\leqslant k\), and let \(f_1, \dotsc, f_m\in \mathfrak{r}(A)\), set \(B=A/\sum_i f_iA\), \(Y=\operatorname{Spec}(B)=V(f_1)\cap\dotsb\cap V(f_m)\), \(Y'=X'\cap Y=Y-\{\mathfrak{a}\}\). Then \(Y\) satisfies the conditions a\(_{k-m}\)), b\(_{k-m}\)). In particular, for every sequence of \(m\leqslant k\) elements \(f_1, \dotsc, f_m\) of \(\mathfrak{r}(A)\), \(Y'=X'\cap V(f_1)\cap\dotsb\cap V(f_m)\) is connected.

It is moreover easy to see that if the last conclusion holds (it evidently suffices to take \(m=k\) in it), and excluding the case where \(X\) would be irreducible of dimension \(0\) or \(1\), it follows that the irreducible components of \(X'\) are of dimension \(\geqslant k+1\), and \(X'\) is connected in dimension \(\geqslant k\), so that in a sense, 2.1 is a "best possible" result.

Let us give the principle of the proof of 2.1. Only the condition b\(_{k-m}\)) for \(Y\) poses a problem. One is easily reduced, for given \(k\), to the case where \(X\) is integral, and even (by passing to the normalization, which is finite over \(X\)) to the case where \(X\) is normal. If \(k=1\), hence \(\dim X' \geqslant 2\), then \(X'\) is of depth \(\geqslant 2\) at its closed points and one can apply the result recalled at the beginning of the N\({}^\circ\), which shows that \(Y'=X'\cap V(f)\) is connected. In the case \(k\geqslant 1\), one supposes the theorem proved for the \(k'\!<\!k\). By induction on \(m\), one is reduced to the case where \(m=1\), i.e. to verifying that for \(f_1\in \mathfrak{r}(A)\), \(X'\cap V(f_1)\) is connected in dimension \(\geqslant k-1\). If it were not, i.e. if it were disconnected by a \(Z'\) of dimension \(< k-1\), there would exist a sequence \(f_2, \dotsc, f_k\) such that \(X'\cap V(f_1)\cap\dotsb\cap V(f_k)\) is disconnected, and in this sequence one can choose arbitrarily \(f_2\in \mathfrak{r}(A)\) subject only to the condition of not vanishing at any point of a certain finite part \(F\) of \(X'\) (namely the set of maximal points of \(Z'\)). Moreover, one verifies easily, using the fact that \(X'\) is normal, hence satisfies Serre's condition (\(S_2\))1 that there exists a finite part \(F'\) of \(X'\) such that \(f\in \mathfrak{r}(A)\), \(V(f)\cap F'=\emptyset\) implies that \(V(f)\cap X'\) likewise satisfies the condition (\(S_2\)). One can then choose \(f_2\) in such a way that \(f_2\) vanishes neither on \(F\) nor on \(F'\), hence that \(X'\cap V(f_2)\) satisfies (\(S_2\)). But then by virtue of HARTSHORNE's theorem III 3.6 \(X'\cap V(f_2)\) is connected in codimension \(1\), hence (since every component of \(X'\cap V(f_2)\) is of dimension \(\geqslant k\)) it is connected in dimension \(\geqslant k-1\). Applying the induction hypothesis to \(V(f_2)=\operatorname{Spec}(A/f_2A)\), it follows that \(X'\cap V(f_2)\cap V(f_1)\cap V(f_3)\cap \dotsb\cap V(f_k)\) is connected, whereas it had been constructed disconnected, which is absurd.

Let us point out a few interesting corollaries:

Corollary 2.2

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a proper morphism of locally noetherian preschemes, with \(Y\) integral, \(y_0\in Y\), \(y_1\) the generic point of \(Y\). One assumes

  1. \(Y\) is unibranch at \(y_0\), every irreducible component of \(X\) dominates \(Y\).

  2. The irreducible components of \(X_1\) are of dimension \(\geqslant k+1\), and \(X_1\) is connected in dimension \(\geqslant k\).

Then the irreducible components of \(X_0\) are of dimension \(\geqslant k+1\), and \(X_0\) is connected in dimension \(\geqslant k\).

Indeed, Zariski's connectedness theorem (cf. EGA III 4.3.1) implies that \(X_0\) is connected; to show that it is not disconnected by a closed subset of dimension \(<k\), one is reduced to showing that the local rings at points \(x\in X_0\) such that \(\dim \overline{x}<k\) have a spectrum not disconnected by \(x\). Now this is true without assuming either \(f\) proper, or \(Y\) unibranch at \(y_0\). To see this one is reduced to the case where \(X\) is integral dominating \(Y\), and if one wishes, \(Y\) affine of finite type over \(\mathbf{Z}\), so that one is under the conditions of the dimension formula for \(\mathscr{O}_{X, x}\) over \(\mathscr{O}_{Y, y_0}\). Using in this case the finiteness of the normal closure, one can even assume \(X\) normal, hence by virtue of a theorem of NAGATA2, the completion of a local ring \(\mathscr{O}_{X, x}\) of \(X'\) is again normal, hence (if \(\mathscr{O}_{X, x}\) is of dimension \(N\)) \(\operatorname{Spec} (\widehat{\mathscr{O}}_{X, x})\) is connected in dimension \(\geqslant N-1\). Let \(n=\dim\mathscr{O}_{X, y_0}\), then \(\operatorname{deg\,tr}~k(x)/k(y)<k\) implies \(\dim \mathscr{O}_{X, x}>n+(k+1)-k=n+1\), taking into account that \(\dim X_1\geqslant k+1\), and taking a system \(f_1, \dotsc, f_n\) of parameters of \(\mathscr{O}_{Y, y_0}\) which one lifts to elements of \(\mathscr{O}_{X, x}\), one sees by 2.1 that \(\operatorname{Spec} (\widehat{\mathscr{O}}_{X, x}/\sum f_i\widehat{\mathscr{O}}_{X, x})\) is connected in dimension \(\geqslant 1\), i.e. is not disconnected by its closed point, or what amounts to the same, \(\operatorname{Spec}(\widehat{\mathscr{O}}_{X_0, x})\) is not disconnected by its closed point; a fortiori the same holds for \(\operatorname{Spec} (\mathscr{O}_{X_0, x})\).

As in the case of the ordinary connectedness theorem, one can vary 2.2 by taking geometric fibres (over the algebraic closures of the residue fields), provided one assumes \(Y\) geometrically unibranch at \(y_0\), or (without any other hypothesis than \(Y\) noetherian) that \(f\) is universally open. Applying this to the case where \(Y\) is the dual scheme of a projective scheme \(\mathbf{P}_k^r\) over a field, one recovers a strengthened form of the global result which had inspired 2.1, namely:

Corollary 2.3

Let \(X\) be a closed subscheme of \(\mathbf{P}^r_k\) (\(k\) a field), one assumes the irreducible components of \(X\) of dimension \(\geqslant k+1\), and \(X\) geometrically connected in dimension \(\geqslant k\). Then for every sequence \(H_1, \dotsc, H_m\) of \(m\) hyperplanes of \(\mathbf{P}^r_k\) (\(0\leqslant m\leqslant k-1\)), \(X\cap H_1\cap \dotsb \cap H_m\) satisfies the same condition with \(k-m\), in particular is geometrically connected in dimension \(\geqslant k-1\).

One can moreover modify in an obvious way this statement, for the case where one is given a proper morphism \(X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbf{P}^r_k\), which is not necessarily an immersion, and an analogous extension is possible for 2.1 (by considering a proper scheme over \(X'\)). These statements moreover follow formally from the statements given here, taking into account the ordinary connectedness theorem which reduces us to the case of a finite morphism.

Corollary 2.4

Let \(A\) be a complete normal noetherian local ring of dimension \(\geqslant k+2\). Let \(X=\operatorname{Spec} (A)\), \(X'=X-\{ \mathfrak{a}\}\), \(f_1, \dotsc, f_k\) elements of \(\mathfrak{r}(A)\), then \(Y'=X'\cap V(f_1) \cap \dotsb \cap V(f_k)\) is connected, and \(\pi_1(Y')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X')\) is surjective.

One proceeds as in SGA 1 X 2.11.

In all of this, only questions of connectedness were involved. Now in the global case, well-known theorems assert that for an irreducible projective variety \(X\subset \mathbf{P}^r_k\), \(k\) algebraically closed, its intersection with a sufficiently "general" hyperplane \(H\) is irreducible, (and not merely connected): this is the BERTINI theorem, proved by ZARISKI, which in turn implies, by Zariski's connectedness theorem, that for every \(H\), \(H\cap X\) is connected (although not necessarily irreducible). One can moreover proceed in the reverse direction, by proving this last result by a Lefschetz-type technique, and deducing the Bertini theorem from it, by reducing to the case where \(X\) is normal, and using the following result: for \(H\) "sufficiently general", \(X\cap H\) is also normal. This suggests the

Conjecture 2.5

Let \(A\) be a complete normal noetherian local ring. Show that there exists \(f\in\mathfrak{r}(A)\) nonzero such that \(Y'=X'\cap V(f)=Y-\{ \mathfrak{a}\}\) (where \(Y=\operatorname{Spec} (A/fA)\)) is normal (hence irreducible by 2.1 if \(\dim A\geqslant 3\)).

To do things properly, one would have to show that in a suitable sense, there even exist "many" elements \(f\) having the property in question, for example that one can choose \(f\) in an arbitrary power of the maximal ideal. Using Serre's normality criterion, and the remark made above for Serre's property \((S_2)\), one sees that one would have an affirmative answer to 2.5 if one had one to the

Conjecture 2.6

Let \(A\) be a complete noetherian local ring, \(U\) an open subset of its spectrum \(X\), \(F\) a finite subset of \(X'=X-\{ \mathfrak{a}\}\). One assumes that \(U\) is regular. Prove that there exists an \(f \in\mathfrak{r}(A)\) such that \(V(f)\cap U\) is regular, and \(V(f)\cap F=\emptyset\).

See, for a result of "local Bertini" type, CHOW [2]

blocks.html: 2 Problems related to \(\pi_0\): local Bertini theorems

Let \(A\) be a complete noetherian local ring, \(f\) an element of its maximal ideal, \(X=\operatorname{Spec} (A)\), \(Y=\operatorname{Spec}(A/fA)\). The use of the local "Lefschetz" technique makes it possible to give criteria for \(Y'=X'\cap Y\) (where \(X'=X-\{\mathfrak{m}\}\)) to be connected, in terms of hypotheses on \(X'\). Thus, it suffices that one have: a) \(X'\) connected b) \(\operatorname{prof} \mathscr{O}_{X', x} \geqslant 2\) for every closed point \(x\) of \(X'\) c) \(f\) is \(A\)-regular. One will note however that hypotheses b) and c) are not of a purely topological nature, for example are not invariant when replacing \(X\) by \(X_{\text{red}}\). In the analogous situation for a projective scheme \(X'\) over a field and a hyperplane section \(Y'\) of \(X'\), the use of the "Bertini theorem" and of Zariski's "connectedness theorem" makes it possible in fact to obtain results of a distinctly more satisfactory appearance, which had led me in the oral seminar to state a conjecture, which I have since resolved in the affirmative. Let us therefore state here:

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 2.1

Let \(A\) be a complete noetherian local ring, \(X\) its spectrum, \(\mathfrak{a}\) the closed point of \(X\), \(X'=X-\{ \mathfrak{a}\}\). Suppose that \(X\) satisfies the conditions (where \(k\) denotes an integer \(\geqslant 1\)):

  • a\(_k\))

    The irreducible components of \(X'\) are of dimension \(\geqslant k+1\).

  • b\(_k\))

    \(X'\) is connected in dimension \(\geqslant k\), i.e. one cannot disconnect \(X'\) by a closed subset of dimension \(<k\) (cf. III 3.8).

Let \(m\) be an integer, \(0\leqslant m\leqslant k\), and let \(f_1, \dotsc, f_m\in \mathfrak{r}(A)\), set \(B=A/\sum_i f_iA\), \(Y=\operatorname{Spec}(B)=V(f_1)\cap\dotsb\cap V(f_m)\), \(Y'=X'\cap Y=Y-\{\mathfrak{a}\}\). Then \(Y\) satisfies the conditions a\(_{k-m}\)), b\(_{k-m}\)). In particular, for every sequence of \(m\leqslant k\) elements \(f_1, \dotsc, f_m\) of \(\mathfrak{r}(A)\), \(Y'=X'\cap V(f_1)\cap\dotsb\cap V(f_m)\) is connected.

It is moreover easy to see that if the last conclusion holds (it evidently suffices to take \(m=k\) in it), and excluding the case where \(X\) would be irreducible of dimension \(0\) or \(1\), it follows that the irreducible components of \(X'\) are of dimension \(\geqslant k+1\), and \(X'\) is connected in dimension \(\geqslant k\), so that in a sense, 2.1 is a "best possible" result.

Let us give the principle of the proof of 2.1. Only the condition b\(_{k-m}\)) for \(Y\) poses a problem. One is easily reduced, for given \(k\), to the case where \(X\) is integral, and even (by passing to the normalization, which is finite over \(X\)) to the case where \(X\) is normal. If \(k=1\), hence \(\dim X' \geqslant 2\), then \(X'\) is of depth \(\geqslant 2\) at its closed points and one can apply the result recalled at the beginning of the N\({}^\circ\), which shows that \(Y'=X'\cap V(f)\) is connected. In the case \(k\geqslant 1\), one supposes the theorem proved for the \(k'\!<\!k\). By induction on \(m\), one is reduced to the case where \(m=1\), i.e. to verifying that for \(f_1\in \mathfrak{r}(A)\), \(X'\cap V(f_1)\) is connected in dimension \(\geqslant k-1\). If it were not, i.e. if it were disconnected by a \(Z'\) of dimension \(< k-1\), there would exist a sequence \(f_2, \dotsc, f_k\) such that \(X'\cap V(f_1)\cap\dotsb\cap V(f_k)\) is disconnected, and in this sequence one can choose arbitrarily \(f_2\in \mathfrak{r}(A)\) subject only to the condition of not vanishing at any point of a certain finite part \(F\) of \(X'\) (namely the set of maximal points of \(Z'\)). Moreover, one verifies easily, using the fact that \(X'\) is normal, hence satisfies Serre's condition (\(S_2\))1 that there exists a finite part \(F'\) of \(X'\) such that \(f\in \mathfrak{r}(A)\), \(V(f)\cap F'=\emptyset\) implies that \(V(f)\cap X'\) likewise satisfies the condition (\(S_2\)). One can then choose \(f_2\) in such a way that \(f_2\) vanishes neither on \(F\) nor on \(F'\), hence that \(X'\cap V(f_2)\) satisfies (\(S_2\)). But then by virtue of HARTSHORNE's theorem III 3.6 \(X'\cap V(f_2)\) is connected in codimension \(1\), hence (since every component of \(X'\cap V(f_2)\) is of dimension \(\geqslant k\)) it is connected in dimension \(\geqslant k-1\). Applying the induction hypothesis to \(V(f_2)=\operatorname{Spec}(A/f_2A)\), it follows that \(X'\cap V(f_2)\cap V(f_1)\cap V(f_3)\cap \dotsb\cap V(f_k)\) is connected, whereas it had been constructed disconnected, which is absurd.
Let us point out a few interesting corollaries:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.2

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a proper morphism of locally noetherian preschemes, with \(Y\) integral, \(y_0\in Y\), \(y_1\) the generic point of \(Y\). One assumes

  1. \(Y\) is unibranch at \(y_0\), every irreducible component of \(X\) dominates \(Y\).

  2. The irreducible components of \(X_1\) are of dimension \(\geqslant k+1\), and \(X_1\) is connected in dimension \(\geqslant k\).

Then the irreducible components of \(X_0\) are of dimension \(\geqslant k+1\), and \(X_0\) is connected in dimension \(\geqslant k\).

Indeed, Zariski's connectedness theorem (cf. EGA III 4.3.1) implies that \(X_0\) is connected; to show that it is not disconnected by a closed subset of dimension \(<k\), one is reduced to showing that the local rings at points \(x\in X_0\) such that \(\dim \overline{x}<k\) have a spectrum not disconnected by \(x\). Now this is true without assuming either \(f\) proper, or \(Y\) unibranch at \(y_0\). To see this one is reduced to the case where \(X\) is integral dominating \(Y\), and if one wishes, \(Y\) affine of finite type over \(\mathbf{Z}\), so that one is under the conditions of the dimension formula for \(\mathscr{O}_{X, x}\) over \(\mathscr{O}_{Y, y_0}\). Using in this case the finiteness of the normal closure, one can even assume \(X\) normal, hence by virtue of a theorem of NAGATA2, the completion of a local ring \(\mathscr{O}_{X, x}\) of \(X'\) is again normal, hence (if \(\mathscr{O}_{X, x}\) is of dimension \(N\)) \(\operatorname{Spec} (\widehat{\mathscr{O}}_{X, x})\) is connected in dimension \(\geqslant N-1\). Let \(n=\dim\mathscr{O}_{X, y_0}\), then \(\operatorname{deg\,tr}~k(x)/k(y)<k\) implies \(\dim \mathscr{O}_{X, x}>n+(k+1)-k=n+1\), taking into account that \(\dim X_1\geqslant k+1\), and taking a system \(f_1, \dotsc, f_n\) of parameters of \(\mathscr{O}_{Y, y_0}\) which one lifts to elements of \(\mathscr{O}_{X, x}\), one sees by 2.1 that \(\operatorname{Spec} (\widehat{\mathscr{O}}_{X, x}/\sum f_i\widehat{\mathscr{O}}_{X, x})\) is connected in dimension \(\geqslant 1\), i.e. is not disconnected by its closed point, or what amounts to the same, \(\operatorname{Spec}(\widehat{\mathscr{O}}_{X_0, x})\) is not disconnected by its closed point; a fortiori the same holds for \(\operatorname{Spec} (\mathscr{O}_{X_0, x})\).

As in the case of the ordinary connectedness theorem, one can vary 2.2 by taking geometric fibres (over the algebraic closures of the residue fields), provided one assumes \(Y\) geometrically unibranch at \(y_0\), or (without any other hypothesis than \(Y\) noetherian) that \(f\) is universally open. Applying this to the case where \(Y\) is the dual scheme of a projective scheme \(\mathbf{P}_k^r\) over a field, one recovers a strengthened form of the global result which had inspired 2.1, namely:

blocks.title: Corollary 2.3
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.3

Let \(X\) be a closed subscheme of \(\mathbf{P}^r_k\) (\(k\) a field), one assumes the irreducible components of \(X\) of dimension \(\geqslant k+1\), and \(X\) geometrically connected in dimension \(\geqslant k\). Then for every sequence \(H_1, \dotsc, H_m\) of \(m\) hyperplanes of \(\mathbf{P}^r_k\) (\(0\leqslant m\leqslant k-1\)), \(X\cap H_1\cap \dotsb \cap H_m\) satisfies the same condition with \(k-m\), in particular is geometrically connected in dimension \(\geqslant k-1\).

One can moreover modify in an obvious way this statement, for the case where one is given a proper morphism \(X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbf{P}^r_k\), which is not necessarily an immersion, and an analogous extension is possible for 2.1 (by considering a proper scheme over \(X'\)). These statements moreover follow formally from the statements given here, taking into account the ordinary connectedness theorem which reduces us to the case of a finite morphism.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.4

Let \(A\) be a complete normal noetherian local ring of dimension \(\geqslant k+2\). Let \(X=\operatorname{Spec} (A)\), \(X'=X-\{ \mathfrak{a}\}\), \(f_1, \dotsc, f_k\) elements of \(\mathfrak{r}(A)\), then \(Y'=X'\cap V(f_1) \cap \dotsb \cap V(f_k)\) is connected, and \(\pi_1(Y')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X')\) is surjective.
One proceeds as in SGA 1 X 2.11.

In all of this, only questions of connectedness were involved. Now in the global case, well-known theorems assert that for an irreducible projective variety \(X\subset \mathbf{P}^r_k\), \(k\) algebraically closed, its intersection with a sufficiently "general" hyperplane \(H\) is irreducible, (and not merely connected): this is the BERTINI theorem, proved by ZARISKI, which in turn implies, by Zariski's connectedness theorem, that for every \(H\), \(H\cap X\) is connected (although not necessarily irreducible). One can moreover proceed in the reverse direction, by proving this last result by a Lefschetz-type technique, and deducing the Bertini theorem from it, by reducing to the case where \(X\) is normal, and using the following result: for \(H\) "sufficiently general", \(X\cap H\) is also normal. This suggests the

blocks.title: Conjecture 2.5
blocks.html:
Conjecture 2.5

Let \(A\) be a complete normal noetherian local ring. Show that there exists \(f\in\mathfrak{r}(A)\) nonzero such that \(Y'=X'\cap V(f)=Y-\{ \mathfrak{a}\}\) (where \(Y=\operatorname{Spec} (A/fA)\)) is normal (hence irreducible by 2.1 if \(\dim A\geqslant 3\)).

To do things properly, one would have to show that in a suitable sense, there even exist "many" elements \(f\) having the property in question, for example that one can choose \(f\) in an arbitrary power of the maximal ideal. Using Serre's normality criterion, and the remark made above for Serre's property \((S_2)\), one sees that one would have an affirmative answer to 2.5 if one had one to the

blocks.title: Conjecture 2.6
blocks.html:
Conjecture 2.6

Let \(A\) be a complete noetherian local ring, \(U\) an open subset of its spectrum \(X\), \(F\) a finite subset of \(X'=X-\{ \mathfrak{a}\}\). One assumes that \(U\) is regular. Prove that there exists an \(f \in\mathfrak{r}(A)\) such that \(V(f)\cap U\) is regular, and \(V(f)\cap F=\emptyset\).
See, for a result of "local Bertini" type, CHOW [2]

footnotes.html: Cf. EGA IV 5.7.2.
Cf. EGA IV 7.8.3 (i) (ii) (v).

pages.title: Problems related to \(\pi_1\)
pages.html:
3 Problems related to \(\pi_1\)

Here again, we have numerous questions, suggested by the global results or the transcendental results.

Conjecture 3.1

Let \(A\) be a complete noetherian local ring with algebraically closed residue field, \(X=\operatorname{Spec} (A)\), \( X'= X- \{\mathfrak{a}\}\), \(\mathfrak{a}\) the closed point. Suppose the irreducible components of \(X\) have dimension \(\geqslant 2\), and \(X'\) connected.

  1. Prove that \(\pi_1(X')\) is topologically finitely generated.

  2. If \(p\) is the characteristic exponent of the residue field \(k\) of \(A\), prove that the largest topological quotient group of \(\pi_1(X')\) which is "of order prime to \(p\)" is finitely presented.

For part (i), using the theory of descent SGA 1 IX 5.2 and theorem 2.4, one is reduced to the case where \(A\) is normal of dimension \(2\). In this case, a systematic method for studying the fundamental group of \(X'\), inaugurated by MUMFORD [5] in the transcendental setting, consists in desingularizing \(X\), i.e. in considering a birational projective morphism \(Z\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), with \(Z\) integral regular, inducing an isomorphism \(Z'=Z|X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X'\); it is plausible that such a \(Z\) always exists, this is in any case what ABHYANKAR's method [1] proves in the case of "equal characteristics"1. Let \(C\) be the fibre of the closed point of \(X\) under \(Z\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), it is an algebraic curve over the residue field \(k\), connected by virtue of the connectedness theorem. The solution of 3.1 then seems related to the

Problem 3.2

With the preceding notations, relate \(\pi_1(X')\) to the topological invariants of \(C\), in particular its fundamental group, (in order to bring out the topological finite generation of \(\pi_1(X')\), using for example SGA X 2.6).

Another method would be to consider \(A\) as a finite algebra over a complete regular local ring \(B\) of dimension \(2\), ramified along a curve \(C\) contained in \(\operatorname{Spec}(B)=Y\). One is thus led to the

Problem 3.3

Let \(A\) be a complete regular local ring of dimension \(2\), with algebraically closed residue field \(k\), \(X\) its spectrum, \(C\) a closed subset of \(X\) of dimension \(1\). Define local invariants of the immersed curve \(C\), having a meaning independent of the residue characteristic, and whose knowledge allows one to compute the fundamental group of \(X-C\) by generators and relations when \(k\) is of characteristic zero. Prove that when \(k\) is of characteristic \(p> 0\), the "tame" fundamental group of \(X-C\) is a quotient of the preceding one, and the two fundamental groups (in char. \(0\), and in char. \(p> 0\)) have the same maximal quotient of order prime to \(p\).

Of course, 3.3 shows us that in 3.1, it is likewise appropriate to replace \(X'\) by a scheme of the form \(X-Y\), where \(Y\) is a closed subset of \(X\) which is of codimension \(\geqslant 2\) in every component of \(X\) containing it. When one drops this restriction on \(Y\), there must again exist an analogous finiteness result, provided one imposes "tame"-type restrictions on the ramification at the maximal points of the irreducible components of \(Y\) which are of codimension \(1\).

Problem 3.4

Let \(A\) be a complete noetherian local ring of dimension \(2\), with algebraically closed residue field. Let again \(X=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), \(X'=X-\{\mathfrak{a}\}\). Find particular structural properties of \(\pi_1(X')\) for the case where \(A\) is a complete intersection.

A satisfactory solution of this problem would perhaps allow one to solve the following old problem:

Conjecture 3.5

Find an irreducible curve in \(\mathbf{P}^3_k\) (\(k\) algebraically closed field), preferably non-singular, which is not set-theoretically the intersection of two hypersurfaces. (KNESER [4] shows that one can always obtain it as the intersection of three hypersurfaces).

pages.title: Problems related to the higher \(\pi_i\): local and global Lefschetz theorems for complex analytic spaces
pages.html:
4 Problems related to the higher \(\pi_i\): local and global Lefschetz theorems for complex analytic spaces

Let \(X\) be a scheme locally of finite type over the field of complex numbers \(\mathbf{C}\); one can associate to it an analytic space \(X^h\) over \(\mathbf{C}\), whence homotopy and homology invariants \(\pi_i(X^h)\), \(\operatorname{H}_i(X^h)\), \(\operatorname{H}^i(X^h)\) etc. ... Moreover, one knows that \(X\) is connected if and only if \(X^h\) is, hence that one has a bijection

\[ \pi_0(X^h)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_0(X) \]

Likewise, since every étale covering \(X'\) of \(X\) defines an étale covering \(X^{\prime h}\) of \(X^h\), one has a canonical homomorphism

\[ \pi_1(X^h)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X), \]

which, using a theorem of Grauert-Remmert, is known to identify the second group with the completion of the first for the topology of subgroups of finite index (which simply expresses the fact that \(X'\rightsquigarrow X^{\prime h}\) is an equivalence of the category of étale coverings of \(X\) with the category of finite étale coverings of \(X^h\)). It follows that the results of this seminar (obtained by purely algebraic means) on \(\pi_0(X)\) and \(\pi_1(X)\) imply results for \(\pi_0(X^h)\) and \(\pi_1(X^h)\) (which are of transcendental nature). If moreover \(X\) is proper, the well-known exact sequence \(0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbf{Z} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbf{C}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbf{C}^*\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0\) makes it possible to show that the Néron-Severi group of \(X\) (the quotient of its Picard group by the connected component of the identity) is isomorphic to a subgroup of \(\operatorname{H}^2(X^h, \mathbf{Z})\); in the nonsingular Kählerian case, it is the subgroup denoted \(\operatorname{H}^{(1, 1)}(X^h, \mathbf{Z})\) (classes of type \((1, 1)\)):

\[ \operatorname{Pic}(X)/\operatorname{Pic}^0(X)\subset \operatorname{H}^2(X, \mathbf{Z}). \]

Consequently, the information we have obtained about Picard groups implies information, very partial it is true, about the groups \(\operatorname{H}^2(X^h, \mathbf{Z})\). It is tempting to complete all these fragmentary results by conjectures.

Very precise indications, going in the same direction as those just pointed out, are provided by a classical theorem of LEFSCHETZ [7]. It asserts that if \(X\) is a nonsingular irreducible projective analytic space of dimension \(n\), and if \(Y\) is a nonsingular hyperplane section, then the injection

\[ Y^{n-1}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X^n \]

induces a homomorphism

\[ \pi_i(Y^{n-1})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_i(X^n) \]

which is an isomorphism for \(i\leqslant n-2\), an epimorphism for \(i=n-1\). Hence the analogous statement for the homomorphisms

\[ \operatorname{H}_i(Y^{n-1})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}_i(X^n) \]

on homology (integral, to fix ideas), while in cohomology,

\[ \operatorname{H}^i(X^n)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}^i(Y^{n-1}) \]

is an isomorphism in dimension \(i\leqslant n-2\), a monomorphism in dimension \(i=n-1\). We have obtained variants of these results in the framework of schemes, for \(\pi_0\), \(\pi_1\), \(\operatorname{Pic}\), valid moreover without nonsingularity hypotheses to a large extent, cf. Exp. XII. Moreover, in eliminating the nonsingularity hypotheses, we made essential use of "local" variants of these global Lefschetz theorems. All this suggests the following problems, which should no doubt be attacked simultaneously1.

Problem 4.1

Let \(X\) be an analytic space, \(Y\) a closed analytic subset of \(X\) (or simply a closed subset?) such that for every \(x\in Y\), the local ring \(\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\) is a complete intersection. Let \(n\) be the complex codimension of \(Y\) in \(X\). Is the canonical homomorphism

\[ \pi_i(X-Y)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_i(X) \]

an isomorphism for \(i\leqslant n-2\), and an epimorphism for \(i=n-1\)?

In this problem and the preceding ones, one obviously implicitly assumes that a base point has been chosen in order to define the homotopy groups. To state the following problem, one must define, for an analytic space \(X\) (more generally, for a locally arcwise connected space) and an \(x\in X\), local invariants \(\pi_i^x(X)\)2. For this, one chooses a non-constant map \(f\) of the interval \([0, 1]\) into \(X\), such that \(f(0)=x\) and \(f(t)\neq x\) for \(t\neq 0\) (such a map exists if \(x\) is not an isolated point). Then for every neighbourhood \(U\) of \(x\), there exists an \(\varepsilon>0\) such that \(0<t<\varepsilon\) implies \(f(t)\in U\), and the homotopy groups \(\pi_i(U-x, f(t))\) are essentially independent of \(t\) (for \(t\) variable, they are related by a transitive system of isomorphisms); they may be denoted \(\pi_i(U-x, f)\). One then sets

\[ \pi_i^x(X)= \varprojlim_{U}\pi_{i-1}(U-x, f) \]

the projective limit being taken over the system of open neighbourhoods \(U\) of \(x\). Strictly speaking, this limit depends on \(f\), and should be denoted \(\pi_i^x(X, f)\), but one checks that for \(f\) variable, these groups are isomorphic to one another3; more precisely they form a local system on the space of paths of the kind considered issuing from \(x\). These invariants are the homotopy version of the local cohomology invariants \(\operatorname{H}^i_x(F)\) for a sheaf \(F\) on \(X\), introduced in I, and should play the role of relative local homotopy groups of \(X\) modulo \(X-x\). Their vanishing for \(i\leqslant n\) and for every \(x\in Y\), where \(Y\) is a closed subset of \(X\) of topological dimension \(\leqslant d\), should imply that the homomorphisms

\[ \pi_i(X-Y) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_i(X) \]

are bijective for \(i<n-d\), and surjective for \(i=n-d\)4. From this point of view, 4.1 would imply (for \(Y\) reduced to a point) a conjecture of purely local nature, expressed by

\[ \pi_i^x(X)=0 \rm{\ for\ } i\leqslant n-1 \]

when \(X\) is a complete intersection of dimension \(n\) at \(x\).

As an example of local invariants \(\pi_i^x(X)\), let us note that if \(x\) is a nonsingular point of complex dimension \(n\), then

\[ \pi_i^x(X)=\pi_{i-1}(S^{2n-1}), \]

where \(S^{2n-1}\) denotes the sphere of dimension \(2n-1\). In particular, in this case \(\pi_{i}^x(X)=0\) for \(i\leqslant 2n-1\), which corresponds to the fact that if from a topological manifold \(X\) one removes a closed subset \(Y\) of codimension \(\geqslant m\), then \(\pi_i(X-Y)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_i(X)\) is an isomorphism for \(i\leqslant m-2\) and an epimorphism for \(i=m-1\).

This being so:

Problem 4.2

Let \(X\) be an analytic space, \(x\in X\), \(t\) a section of \(\mathscr{O}_X\) vanishing at \(x\), \(Y\) the set of zeros of \(t\). Suppose the following conditions are satisfied:

  1. \(t\) is regular at \(x\) (i.e. not a zero divisor of \(0\) at \(x\), a hypothesis which is perhaps superfluous, moreover).

  2. At the points \(x'\) of \(X-Y\) near \(x\), \(\mathscr{O}_{X, x'}\) is a complete intersection (a hypothesis which should be replaceable by the following more general one if 4.1 is true: for \(x'\) as above, \(\pi_i^{x'}(X)=0\) for \(i\leqslant n-1\)).

  3. At the points \(y\) of \(Y-\{ x\}\) near \(x\), one has

    \[ \operatorname{prof} \mathscr{O}_{X, y}\geqslant n \]

    (it suffices for example that one has \(\operatorname{prof}\mathscr{O}_{X, x}\geqslant n\)).

Under these conditions, is the canonical homomorphism

\[ \pi_i^x(Y)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_i^x(X) \]

an isomorphism for \(i\leqslant n-2\), an epimorphism for \(i=n-1\)?

@@@TITLE:0@@@ Problem

Here, finally, is a global variant of 4.2, which should be deducible from it by consideration of the projecting cone at its vertex, and which would generalize the classical Lefschetz theorems:

Problem 4.3

Let \(X\) be a projective analytic space, equipped with an ample invertible Module \(L\), \(t\) a section of \(L\), \(Y\) the set of zeros of \(t\). Suppose:

  1. \(t\) is a regular section (perhaps superfluous).

  2. For every \(x\in X-Y\), \(\mathscr{O}_{X, x}\) is a complete intersection (should be replaceable by \(\pi_i^{x}(X)=0\) for \(i\leqslant n-1\)).

  3. For every \(x\in Y\), \(\operatorname{prof} \mathscr{O}_{X, x}\geqslant n\).

Under these conditions, is the homomorphism

\[ \pi_i(Y)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_i(X) \]

an isomorphism for \(i\leqslant n-2\), an epimorphism for \(i=n-1\)?

We leave to the reader the task of stating analogous conjectures of cohomological nature5, the hypotheses and conclusions then bearing on the local cohomological invariants (with coefficients in a given group). In any case, the key result seems bound to be 4.2, when hypothesis \(b)\) is taken there in resp.-form, — whether one places oneself from the point of view of homology, or of homotopy.

We have stated these conjectures in the transcendental framework, in the hope of interesting topologists in them and convincing them that questions of "Lefschetz" type are far from closed. Of course, now that we are about to have at our disposal a good theory of the cohomology of schemes (with finite coefficients), thanks to the recent work of M. ARTIN, the same questions arise in the framework of schemes, and it is difficult to doubt that they will receive a positive answer, in the near future6.

blocks.title: Problem
blocks.html: 4 Problems related to the higher \(\pi_i\): local and global Lefschetz theorems for complex analytic spaces

Let \(X\) be a scheme locally of finite type over the field of complex numbers \(\mathbf{C}\); one can associate to it an analytic space \(X^h\) over \(\mathbf{C}\), whence homotopy and homology invariants \(\pi_i(X^h)\), \(\operatorname{H}_i(X^h)\), \(\operatorname{H}^i(X^h)\) etc. ... Moreover, one knows that \(X\) is connected if and only if \(X^h\) is, hence that one has a bijection
\[ \pi_0(X^h)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_0(X) \]
Likewise, since every étale covering \(X'\) of \(X\) defines an étale covering \(X^{\prime h}\) of \(X^h\), one has a canonical homomorphism
\[ \pi_1(X^h)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X), \]

which, using a theorem of Grauert-Remmert, is known to identify the second group with the completion of the first for the topology of subgroups of finite index (which simply expresses the fact that \(X'\rightsquigarrow X^{\prime h}\) is an equivalence of the category of étale coverings of \(X\) with the category of finite étale coverings of \(X^h\)). It follows that the results of this seminar (obtained by purely algebraic means) on \(\pi_0(X)\) and \(\pi_1(X)\) imply results for \(\pi_0(X^h)\) and \(\pi_1(X^h)\) (which are of transcendental nature). If moreover \(X\) is proper, the well-known exact sequence \(0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbf{Z} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbf{C}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbf{C}^*\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0\) makes it possible to show that the Néron-Severi group of \(X\) (the quotient of its Picard group by the connected component of the identity) is isomorphic to a subgroup of \(\operatorname{H}^2(X^h, \mathbf{Z})\); in the nonsingular Kählerian case, it is the subgroup denoted \(\operatorname{H}^{(1, 1)}(X^h, \mathbf{Z})\) (classes of type \((1, 1)\)):
\[ \operatorname{Pic}(X)/\operatorname{Pic}^0(X)\subset \operatorname{H}^2(X, \mathbf{Z}). \]

Consequently, the information we have obtained about Picard groups implies information, very partial it is true, about the groups \(\operatorname{H}^2(X^h, \mathbf{Z})\). It is tempting to complete all these fragmentary results by conjectures.

Very precise indications, going in the same direction as those just pointed out, are provided by a classical theorem of LEFSCHETZ [7]. It asserts that if \(X\) is a nonsingular irreducible projective analytic space of dimension \(n\), and if \(Y\) is a nonsingular hyperplane section, then the injection
\[ Y^{n-1}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X^n \]
induces a homomorphism
\[ \pi_i(Y^{n-1})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_i(X^n) \]
which is an isomorphism for \(i\leqslant n-2\), an epimorphism for \(i=n-1\). Hence the analogous statement for the homomorphisms
\[ \operatorname{H}_i(Y^{n-1})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}_i(X^n) \]
on homology (integral, to fix ideas), while in cohomology,
\[ \operatorname{H}^i(X^n)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}^i(Y^{n-1}) \]

is an isomorphism in dimension \(i\leqslant n-2\), a monomorphism in dimension \(i=n-1\). We have obtained variants of these results in the framework of schemes, for \(\pi_0\), \(\pi_1\), \(\operatorname{Pic}\), valid moreover without nonsingularity hypotheses to a large extent, cf. Exp. XII. Moreover, in eliminating the nonsingularity hypotheses, we made essential use of "local" variants of these global Lefschetz theorems. All this suggests the following problems, which should no doubt be attacked simultaneously1.

pages.title: Problems related to local Picard groups
pages.html:
5 Problems related to local Picard groups

A first fundamental problem, pointed out for the first time by MUMFORD [5] in a particular case, is the following. Let \(A\) be a complete local ring with residue field \(k\), \(X=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), \(U=\operatorname{Spec}(A)-\{a\}\), where \(a\) is the maximal ideal of \(A\), i.e. the closed point of \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\). We propose to construct a strict projective system \(G\) of locally algebraic groups \(G_i\) over \(k\), and a natural isomorphism

\[ \tag{+} {\operatorname{Pic}(U)\simeq G(k)} \]

where one of course sets \(G(k)=\varprojlim G_i(k)\). Heuristically, we propose to "put an algebraic group structure" (or, at least, a pro-algebraic one, in a suitable sense) on the group \(\operatorname{Pic}(X')\).

It is evident that, as it stands, the problem is not precise enough, for the datum of an isomorphism (+) is far from characterizing the pro-object \(G\). If \(A\) contains a subfield, again denoted by \(k\), which is a field of representatives, one can make the problem precise by requiring that, for a variable extension \(k'\) of \(k\), one have an isomorphism, functorial in \(k'\):

\[ \tag{+'} {\operatorname{Pic}(U')\simeq G(k')} \]

where \(U'\) is the open set analogous to \(U\) in \(\operatorname{Spec}(A')\), \(A'=A\widehat{\otimes}_k k'\). One can proceed in an analogous way even if \(A\) has no field of representatives, provided that \(k\) be perfect, which then allows one to construct functorially an \(A'\) "by residual extension \(k'/k\)". Moreover, when \(A\) admits a field of representatives, the algebraic structure that one finds on \(\operatorname{Pic}(U)\) will depend essentially on the choice of this field of representatives (as one already sees on the projecting cone of an elliptic curve); it therefore seems that one must start from a "pro-algebraic ring over \(k\)" à la GREENBERG [3], in order to manage to define the pro-object \(G\). It is moreover conceivable that, in the case where there is no given field of representatives, one finds only a projective system of quasi-algebraic groups in the sense of Serre, or rather quasi-locally algebraic ones (the groups \(G_i\) obtained will not in general be of finite type over \(k\), but only locally of finite type over \(k\)). It is even possible that one will in general find only an even weaker structure on \(\operatorname{Pic}(U)\), of the kind encountered by NERON [6] in his theory of degeneration of abelian varieties defined over local fields.

One method for attacking the problem, also introduced by MUMFORD, consists in desingularizing \(X\), i.e. in considering a projective birational morphism \(Y\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) with \(Y\) regular. When \(U\) is regular (i.e. \(a\) is an isolated singular point), one can often find \(Y\) in such a way that \(Y|U=V\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} U\) is an isomorphism. In this case, one will therefore have

\[ \operatorname{Pic}(U)\simeq\operatorname{Pic}(V)\simeq\operatorname{Pic}(Y)/\operatorname{Im} \mathbf{Z}^I, \]

where \(I\) is the set of irreducible components of the fibre \(Y_{a}\), (each of these defining an element of \(\operatorname{Pic}(Y)\), being a locally principal divisor, thanks to \(Y\) regular). On the other hand, using the technique of Formal Geometry EGA III 4 and 5, in particular the existence theorem, one finds

\[ \operatorname{Pic}(Y)\simeq\varprojlim\operatorname{Pic}(Y_n), \]

where \(Y_n=Y\otimes_A A_n\), \(A_n=A/\mathfrak{m}^{n+1}\). When \(A\) admits a field of representatives \(k\), one has at one's disposal the theory of Picard schemes of the projective schemes \(Y_n\) over \(k\), so one has

\[ \operatorname{Pic}(Y_n)\simeq \mathbf{Pic}_{Y_n/k}(k). \]

This therefore provides a construction of a projective system of locally algebraic groups \(\mathbf{Pic}_{Y_n/k}/\operatorname{Im} \mathbf{Z}^I\), which is the system sought. In the case considered here, one can moreover see (using that \(a\) is an isolated singular point) that the connected components of the universal image subgroups in this projective system form an essentially constant projective system, so that in this case one finds a locally algebraic group \(G\) as the solution of the problem. If one even assumes \(A\) normal of dimension \(2\), then a remark of MUMFORD (stating that the intersection matrix of the components of \(Y_{a}\) in \(X\) is negative definite) implies that \(G\) is even an algebraic group, i.e. of finite type over \(k\) (the number of its connected components being moreover equal to the determinant of the intersection matrix considered a moment ago).

If on the contrary \(a\) is not an isolated singularity, one convinces oneself on examples (with \(A\) of dimension \(2\)) that one finds a projective system of algebraic groups, not reducing to a single algebraic group.

Once one had at one's disposal a good notion of "local Picard scheme", there would be reason to strengthen the notion of parafactoriality, by saying that \(A\) is "geometrically parafactorial" when not only \(A\) and even \(\widehat{A}\) are parafactorial, but the local Picard scheme \(G(\widehat{A})\) is the trivial group (which is stronger, when the residue field is not algebraically closed, than to say that \(G\) has no rational point over \(k\) other than the identity). One realizes the necessity of a strengthened notion of parafactoriality by recalling that there exist complete normal local rings of dimension \(2\) which are factorial, but which admit finite étale algebras that are not. A "geometrically factorial" local ring would then be a normal ring \(A\) such that all its localizations of dimension \(\geqslant 2\) are geometrically parafactorial, or better, such that the localizations of \(\widehat{A}\) are parafactorial1. Of course, it would be interesting to find a "good" definition of these notions, independent of the theory, still to be developed, of local Picard schemes.

It is in any case plausible that one will need these notions if one wishes to obtain statements of the following type: Let \(A\) be a "good ring" (for example an algebra of finite type over \(\mathbf{Z}\), or over a complete local ring, for example over a field). Let \(U\) be the set of \(x\in X=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) such that \(\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\) is "geometrically factorial"; then is \(U\) open? Or again: Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a flat morphism of finite type with \(Y\) locally noetherian, let \(U\) be the set of \(x\in X\) such that \(\mathscr{O}_{X_{f(x)}, x}\) is "geometrically factorial"; then is \(U\) open, at least under suitable additional conditions on \(f\)? I doubt that, with the usual notion of factorial ring, there exist true statements of this type.

We have raised here, in a particular case, the question of the study of the geometric properties of "variable" local rings, for example the \(\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\) for \(x\) ranging over a prescheme \(X\). When \(X\) is a scheme of finite type over a field, for example, it is known that there exists on \(X\) a projective system of finite algebras \(P^n_{X/k}\) (obtained by completing \(X\times_k X\) along the diagonal), whose fibre at every point \(x\in X\) rational over \(k\) is isomorphic to the projective system of the \(\mathscr{O}_{X,x}/\mathfrak{m}_X^{n+1}\). It is then natural to relate the study of the completions of the local rings \(\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\), for \(x\) variable, to that of the "algebraic family of complete local rings" given by the \(P^n\), noting that for every \(x\in X\) (rational over \(k\) or not), one obtains a complete local ring

\[ P^{\infty}(x)=\varprojlim P^n(x) \]

(where \(P^n(x)=\) reduced fibre \(P^n\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{X,x}}k(x)\)). A particular interest will attach for example to the complete ring thus associated to the generic point, and one will expect that its algebraic-geometric properties (expressed for example by its local Picard groups, or homotopy groups, or homology groups) will be essentially those of the completions \(\widehat{\mathscr{O}}_{X, x}\) for \(x\) in a suitable dense open set \(U\).

One can, in a general way, propose to carry out the simultaneous study of the complete local rings thus obtained from an adic projective system \((P_n)\) of finite algebras over a given scheme \(X\). It is plausible that one will find, subject to certain regularity conditions (such as the flatness of the \(P_n\)), that the local homotopy groups come from a projective system of finite group schemes over \(X\), and that one will have analogous results for the local Picard groups. As regards the latter, a first interesting case that deserves to be investigated is the one where one starts from an algebraic surface \(X\) having singular curves, and one proposes to study the local Picard schemes at the variable points on these, in terms of a suitable pro-group scheme defined over the singular locus.

where \(Y_n=Y\otimes_A A_n\), \(A_n=A/\mathfrak{m}^{n+1}\). When \(A\) admits a field of representatives \(k\), one has at one's disposal the theory of Picard schemes of the projective schemes \(Y_n\) over \(k\), so one has
\[ \operatorname{Pic}(Y_n)\simeq \mathbf{Pic}_{Y_n/k}(k). \]

This therefore provides a construction of a projective system of locally algebraic groups \(\mathbf{Pic}_{Y_n/k}/\operatorname{Im} \mathbf{Z}^I\), which is the system sought. In the case considered here, one can moreover see (using that \(a\) is an isolated singular point) that the connected components of the universal image subgroups in this projective system form an essentially constant projective system, so that in this case one finds a locally algebraic group \(G\) as the solution of the problem. If one even assumes \(A\) normal of dimension \(2\), then a remark of MUMFORD (stating that the intersection matrix of the components of \(Y_{a}\) in \(X\) is negative definite) implies that \(G\) is even an algebraic group, i.e. of finite type over \(k\) (the number of its connected components being moreover equal to the determinant of the intersection matrix considered a moment ago).

If on the contrary \(a\) is not an isolated singularity, one convinces oneself on examples (with \(A\) of dimension \(2\)) that one finds a projective system of algebraic groups, not reducing to a single algebraic group.

Once one had at one's disposal a good notion of "local Picard scheme", there would be reason to strengthen the notion of parafactoriality, by saying that \(A\) is "geometrically parafactorial" when not only \(A\) and even \(\widehat{A}\) are parafactorial, but the local Picard scheme \(G(\widehat{A})\) is the trivial group (which is stronger, when the residue field is not algebraically closed, than to say that \(G\) has no rational point over \(k\) other than the identity). One realizes the necessity of a strengthened notion of parafactoriality by recalling that there exist complete normal local rings of dimension \(2\) which are factorial, but which admit finite étale algebras that are not. A "geometrically factorial" local ring would then be a normal ring \(A\) such that all its localizations of dimension \(\geqslant 2\) are geometrically parafactorial, or better, such that the localizations of \(\widehat{A}\) are parafactorial1. Of course, it would be interesting to find a "good" definition of these notions, independent of the theory, still to be developed, of local Picard schemes.

It is in any case plausible that one will need these notions if one wishes to obtain statements of the following type: Let \(A\) be a "good ring" (for example an algebra of finite type over \(\mathbf{Z}\), or over a complete local ring, for example over a field). Let \(U\) be the set of \(x\in X=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) such that \(\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\) is "geometrically factorial"; then is \(U\) open? Or again: Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a flat morphism of finite type with \(Y\) locally noetherian, let \(U\) be the set of \(x\in X\) such that \(\mathscr{O}_{X_{f(x)}, x}\) is "geometrically factorial"; then is \(U\) open, at least under suitable additional conditions on \(f\)? I doubt that, with the usual notion of factorial ring, there exist true statements of this type.

We have raised here, in a particular case, the question of the study of the geometric properties of "variable" local rings, for example the \(\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\) for \(x\) ranging over a prescheme \(X\). When \(X\) is a scheme of finite type over a field, for example, it is known that there exists on \(X\) a projective system of finite algebras \(P^n_{X/k}\) (obtained by completing \(X\times_k X\) along the diagonal), whose fibre at every point \(x\in X\) rational over \(k\) is isomorphic to the projective system of the \(\mathscr{O}_{X,x}/\mathfrak{m}_X^{n+1}\). It is then natural to relate the study of the completions of the local rings \(\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\), for \(x\) variable, to that of the "algebraic family of complete local rings" given by the \(P^n\), noting that for every \(x\in X\) (rational over \(k\) or not), one obtains a complete local ring
\[ P^{\infty}(x)=\varprojlim P^n(x) \]

(where \(P^n(x)=\) reduced fibre \(P^n\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{X,x}}k(x)\)). A particular interest will attach for example to the complete ring thus associated to the generic point, and one will expect that its algebraic-geometric properties (expressed for example by its local Picard groups, or homotopy groups, or homology groups) will be essentially those of the completions \(\widehat{\mathscr{O}}_{X, x}\) for \(x\) in a suitable dense open set \(U\).

One can, in a general way, propose to carry out the simultaneous study of the complete local rings thus obtained from an adic projective system \((P_n)\) of finite algebras over a given scheme \(X\). It is plausible that one will find, subject to certain regularity conditions (such as the flatness of the \(P_n\)), that the local homotopy groups come from a projective system of finite group schemes over \(X\), and that one will have analogous results for the local Picard groups. As regards the latter, a first interesting case that deserves to be investigated is the one where one starts from an algebraic surface \(X\) having singular curves, and one proposes to study the local Picard schemes at the variable points on these, in terms of a suitable pro-group scheme defined over the singular locus.
