6 Comments

The point of view of the "Étale Cohomology" of schemes and recent progress in this theory leads us to make precise and at the same time to broaden some of the problems raised. For the notion of "topology" and of "étale topology of a scheme", I refer to M. ARTIN, Grothendieck Topologies, Harvard University 1962 (mimeographed notes)1.

This theory, through a finer notion of localization than the one provided by the traditional "Zariski topology", leads one to attach particular interest to strictly local rings, i.e. the henselian local rings with separably closed residue field. For any local ring \(A\) with residue field \(k\), and any separable closure \(k'\) of \(k\), one can find a local homomorphism from \(A\) into a strictly local ring \(A'\), the strictly local closure of \(A\), with residue field \(k'\), having an obvious universal property. \(A'\) is henselian, flat over \(A\), and \(A'\otimes_A k\simeq k'\); it is noetherian if and only if \(A\) is. (Cf. loc. cit. Chap. III, section 4)2. If \(X\) is a prescheme, and \(x\) a point of \(X\), \(x'\) a point above \(x\), the spectrum of a separable closure \(k'\) of \(k=k(x)\), one is led to define the strictly local ring of \(X\) at \(x'\), \(\mathscr{O}'_{X, x'}\), as the strictly local closure of the usual local ring \(\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\), relative to the residual extension \(K'/K\). It is the strictly local rings of the "geometric" points of \(X\) which, from the point of view of the étale topology, are meant to reflect the local properties of the prescheme \(X\). They also play, in many respects, the role that was assigned to the completions of the local rings of \(X\) (say, at the points with algebraically closed residue field), while remaining "closer" to \(X\) and allowing an easier passage to the "neighbouring points".

There is then reason to take up again a good number of questions, which are generally posed for complete local rings (possibly restricted to having an algebraically closed residue field), for henselian noetherian local rings (resp. noetherian strictly local rings). It is thus that the topological problems raised in n\(^{\textup{os}}\) 2 and 3 are posed more generally for strictly local rings. One can moreover state conjecturally, for "good" strictly local rings, certain properties of simple connectedness and of acyclicity for the geometric fibres of the canonical morphism \(\operatorname{Spec}(\widehat{A})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), which would show that for many properties of a "topological" nature, it amounts to the same thing to prove them for the ring \(A\), or for its completion \(\widehat{A}\). Certain results already obtained along these lines3 give grounds to hope that complete results in this direction will soon be available.

The notion of étale localization provides a definition, which seems reasonable, of the notion of a "geometrically parafactorial" or "geometrically factorial" local ring (the need for which was pointed out in n° 5, p. 20): one will thus call a local ring whose strictly local closure is parafactorial, resp. factorial. Hypotheses of this nature do indeed arise naturally in the study of the étale cohomology of preschemes. Thus, if \(X\) is a locally noetherian prescheme whose strictly local rings are factorial (i.e. whose ordinary local rings are "geometrically factorial"), one shows that the \(\operatorname{H}^i(X_{{\textup{ét}}}, \mathbf{G}_m)\) are torsion groups for \(i\geqslant 2\) (which sometimes makes it possible to express these groups in terms of the cohomology groups with coefficients in the groups \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_n\) of \(n\)th roots of unity), and if \(X\) is integral with field of fractions \(K\), the natural homomorphism \(\operatorname{H}^2(X_{{\textup{ét}}}, \mathbf{G}_m)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}^2(K, \mathbf{G}_m)=\operatorname{Br}(K)\) is injective; examples show that these conclusions may fail, even for \(X\) local, if one assumes only that \(X\) is factorial instead of geometrically factorial4.

Concerning the problems of local and global Lefschetz type raised in 3.4, and their analogues in scheme theory, the homological version of these questions has been considerably clarified, everything following formally from three general theorems, one concerning the cohomological dimension of certain affine schemes (resp. of Stein spaces), such as the affine schemes \(X\) of finite type over an algebraically closed field: their cohomological dimension is \(\leqslant\dim X\) ("affine Lefschetz theorem")5; the other being a duality theorem for the cohomology (with discrete coefficients) of a projective morphism6, and finally the last a local duality theorem of an analogous nature7. In Algebraic Geometry, only this last one is not proved at the moment of writing these lines (it is however proved in char. \(0\), using the resolution of singularities by HIRONAKA). Moreover, in the transcendental setting, one has at present both the global and local duality, recently proved by VERDIER. Let us limit ourselves to indicating that in the statement of the homological versions of problems 4.2 and 4.3 (which from now on deserve the name of conjectures), the conditions "at infinity" a) and c) are certainly superfluous, the only important thing being the local cohomological structure of \(X-Y\), which one will assume for example to be locally a complete intersection of dimension \(\geqslant n\). Moreover, in 4.3 say, the fact that \(Y\) is a hyperplane section ought not to matter, and should be replaceable by the sole hypothesis that \(X\) is compact and \(X-Y\) is Stein (i.e. in the case of Algebraic Geometry, \(X\) is proper over \(k\) and \(X-Y\) affine; as we said, the homological version of this conjecture is proved for algebraic spaces over the field \(\mathbf{C}\))8.

In the definition (p. 15) of the \(\pi_i^x(X)\), one must assume \(i\geqslant 2\). For \(i=0, 1\), there is no reasonable definition of the \(\pi_i^x(X)\), and there is reason to replace them by \(\operatorname{H}_0^x(X)\) and \(\operatorname{H}_1^x(X)\), defined respectively as the cokernel and the kernel in the natural homomorphism

\[ \varprojlim \operatorname{H}_0(U-(x), \mathbf{Z})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\varprojlim\operatorname{H}_0(U, \mathbf{Z}). \]

At a pinch and for the convenience of formulations, one may set \(\pi_i^x(X)=\operatorname{H}_i^x(X)\) for \(i\leqslant 1\); otherwise one must supplement the later assertions concerning the \(\pi_i^x\) by the corresponding assertions for \(\operatorname{H}_0^x, \operatorname{H}_1^x\). If \(x\) is an isolated point of \(X\), it is appropriate to set \(\pi_i^x(X)=0\) for \(i\neq 0\), \(\pi_0^x(X)=\operatorname{H}_0^x(X)=\mathbf{Z}\).

The assertion that the \(\pi_i^x(U, f)\) are isomorphic to one another is true only when \(X\) is not disconnected by \(x\) in a neighbourhood of \(x\), i.e. if \(\pi_i^x(X)=0\) for \(i=0, 1\). In the general case \(\pi_i^x(X)\) can denote only a family of groups, not necessarily isomorphic to one another; nevertheless the notation \(\pi_i^x(X)=0\) keeps an obvious meaning.

Page 15, line - 11, where I predict that the vanishing of the local homotopy invariants \(\pi_i^x(X)\) for \(x\in Y\), \(i\leqslant n\) must entail the bijectivity of \(\pi_i(X-Y)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_i(X)\) for \(i<n-d\), and the surjectivity for \(i=n-d\), one should be cautious, for lack of being able to dispose, in the present context (as in Algebraic Geometry), of "general" points at which the local conditions will also have to apply. It will doubtless be necessary, for this reason, to appeal to relative local homotopy invariants

\[ \pi_i^Y(X, f)=\pi_i^Y(X, x)=\varprojlim_U \pi_{i-1}(U-U\cap Y, f(t))\qquad\text{for }i\geqslant 2, \]

(and an ad-hoc definition as above for \(i=0, 1\)), where \(Y\) is a closed part of \(X\); or to make up for the absence of general points by expressing the hypotheses on \(X\) in terms of properties of a topological nature (for the étale topology) of the spectra of the local rings of \(X\), which makes it possible to recover general points. The same reservation applies to the generalization of conjectures 4.2 and 4.3 to the case where \(X-Y\) is not assumed to be locally a complete intersection, a generalization suggested in the statement of the conditions b) of these conjectures.

In order to formulate the expurgated versions of conjectures 4.2 and 4.3 suggested by the results alluded to above, it is appropriate to lay down the

Definition 1

Let \(X\) be a topological space, \(Y\) a locally closed part of \(X\), and \(n\) an integer. One says that \(X\) is of homotopy depth \(\geqslant n\) along \(Y\), and one writes \(\mathrm{prof\:hpt}_Y(X)\geqslant n\), if for every \(x\in Y\), one has \(\pi_i^Y(X, x)=0\) for \(i<n\).

It ought to be equivalent to say that for every open set \(X'\) of \(X\), and every \(x\in X'\cap U=U'\) (where \(U=X-Y\)), the canonical homomorphism

\[ \pi_i(U', x)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_i(X', x) \]

is an isomorphism for \(i<n-1\), a monomorphism for \(i=n-1\).

Definition 2

Let \(X\) be a complex analytic space, \(n\) an integer; one says that the rectified homotopy depth of \(X\) is \(n\)9, if for every locally closed analytic part \(Y\) of \(X\), one has

\[ \tag{x} {\mathrm{prof\:htp}_Y(X)\geqslant n-\dim Y} \]

(where, of course, \(\dim Y\) denotes the complex dimension of \(Y\)).

It ought to be equivalent to say that for every irreducible analytic part \(Y\) locally closed in \(X\), there exists a closed analytic part \(Z\) of \(Y\), of dimension \(<\dim Y\), such that the relation (x) holds for \(Y-Z\) instead of \(Y\). This would make it possible for example in definition 2 to restrict to the case where \(Y\) is non-singular.

The following conjecture, of a purely topological nature, is in the nature of a "local Hurewicz theorem".

Conjecture A

["local Hurewicz th."] Let \(X\) be a topological space, \(Y\) a locally closed part, subject if need be to conditions of "smoothness" of the kind of local triangulability of the pair \((X, Y)\), \(n\) an integer \(\geqslant 3\). In order to have \(\mathrm{prof\:htp}_Y(X)\geqslant n\), it is necessary and sufficient to have

\[ \operatorname{H}^i_Y(\mathbf{Z}_X)=0\quad\text{for } i<n \]

(one then says that \(X\) is of cohomological depth \(\geqslant n\) along \(Y\)), and that the local fundamental groups

\[ \pi^2_Y(X, x)=\varprojlim_{U\ni x} \pi_1(U-U\cap Y) \]

be zero (one then says that \(X\) is "pure" along \(Y\)).

One will note that if \(X\) is an analytic space, \(Y\) an analytic subspace, and if \(X\) is pure along \(Y\), then for every \(x\in Y\), the local ring \(\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\), as well as its localizations with respect to prime ideals containing the ideal defining the germ \(Y\) at \(x\) (i.e. lying in the inverse image \(Y_x\) of \(Y\) under \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_{X,x})=X_x\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\)) are pure in the sense of Exp. X; it seems plausible that the converse is also true. Analogous remarks hold for the cohomological depth, it being understood that one works with the étale topology on the \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_{X,x})\).

Conjecture 4.1 then generalizes into the

Conjecture B

["Purity"] Let \(E\) be an analytic space, \(X\) an analytic part of \(E\). Assume that \(E\) is non-singular of dimension \(N\) at \(x\), and that \(X\) can be described by \(p\) analytic equations in a neighbourhood of every point. Then the rectified homotopy depth of \(X\) is \(\geqslant N-p\).

In particular, a local complete intersection of dimension \(n\) at every point would be of rectified homotopy depth \(\geqslant n\), which is none other than conjecture 4.1.

Conjectures 4.2 and 4.3 generalize respectively into:

Conjecture C

["local Lefschetz"] Let \(X\) be an analytic space, \(Y\) a closed analytic part, \(x\) a point of \(Y\); assume that \(X-Y\) is Stein in a neighbourhood of \(x\) (for example \(Y\) defined by one equation at \(x\)), and that \(X-Y\) is of rectified homotopy depth \(\geqslant n\) in a neighbourhood of \(x\) (for example, is at every point of \(X-Y\) near \(x\) a complete intersection of dimension \(\geqslant n\), cf. conjecture B). Then the canonical homomorphism

\[ \pi_i^x(Y)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_i^x(X) \]

is an isomorphism for \(i<n-1\), an epimorphism for \(i=n-1\).

Conjecture D

["global Lefschetz"]

Let \(X\) be a compact analytic space, \(Y\) an analytic subspace of \(X\) such that \(U=X-Y\) is Stein, and is of rectified homotopy depth \(\geqslant n\) (for example a complete intersection of dimension \(\geqslant n\) at every point). Then the canonical homomorphism

\[ \pi_i(Y)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_i(X) \]

is an isomorphism for \(i<n-1\), an epimorphism for \(i=n-1\).

Remark

When, in statements C and D, one replaces the hypothesis that \(X-Y\) is Stein by the hypothesis that \(X-Y\) is a union of \(c+1\) Stein open sets (which will play the role of a topological "concavity" hypothesis), the conclusions must be modified simply by replacing therein \(n\) by \(n-c\).

Let us make explicit to finish, in the "global case" D, the conjecture concerning the fundamental group (obtained by taking \(n=3\)):

Conjecture D\(\boldsymbol'\)

[global Lefschetz for fundamental group]

Let \(X\) be a compact analytic space over the field of complex numbers, \(Y\) a closed analytic part such that \(U=X-Y\) is Stein. Assume moreover that the following conditions are satisfied:

  1. For every \(x\in U\), the local fundamental group \(\pi_2^x(X, x)\) is zero (i.e. \(X\) is "pure at \(x\)"), or merely the local ring \(\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\) is pure.

  2. The local rings of the points of \(U\) are "connected in dimension \(\geqslant 2\)".

  3. The local rings of the points of \(U\) are of dimension \(\geqslant 3\).

Under these conditions, for every \(x\in Y\), the homomorphism

\[ \pi_1(Y, x)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1(X, x) \]

is an isomorphism (and \(\pi_2(Y, x)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_2(X, x)\) an epimorphism).

One will note that the local conditions (i) (ii) (iii) on \(U\) are satisfied if \(U\) is locally a complete intersection of dimension \(\geqslant 3\). From the point of view of Algebraic Geometry (when \(U\) comes from a scheme, still denoted \(U\)), conditions (i) to (iii) correspond to hypotheses on the local invariants \(\pi_i^x(U)\), namely \(\pi_i^x(U)=0\) for \(i<3-\operatorname{deg\,tr} k(x)/k\), for the points \(x\) such that one has respectively \(\operatorname{deg\,tr} k(x)/k=0, 1, 2\). The global condition on \(U\) (\(U\) Stein) will be satisfied if \(X\) is projective and \(Y\) a hyperplane section.

[1]
S. Abhyankar, Local uniformisation on algebraic surfaces, Annals of Math. vol. 63, 3, 1956 ↩
[2]
W.L. Chow, On the theorem of Bertini for local domains, Proc. Nat. Acad. Sc. Vol. 44, 6, pp. 580–584, 1958 ↩
[3]
M. Greenberg, Schemata over local rings, Annals of Math., vol 73, 1961 ↩
[4]
M. Kneser, Uber die Darstellung algebraischer Raumkurven als Durchschnitte von Flächen, Archiv der Math. Vol. XI, fasc. 3, 1960 ↩
[5]
D. Mumford, The topology of normal singularities of an algebraic surface, and a criterion for simplicity, Publications Mathématiques 9, 1961 ↩
[6]
A. Néron, Modèles minimaux des variétés abéliennes sur les corps locaux et globaux, à paraître aux Publications Mathématiques (existe en notes miméographiées à circulation restreinte...) ↩
[7]
A.H. Wallace, Homology Theory of algebraic Varieties, Pergamon Press, 1958. ↩
[8]
S. Abhyankar, Resolution of singularities of arithmetical surfaces, p. 111–152, Arithmetical Algebraic Geometry, Harper and Row, New-York 1963. ↩

It will doubtless be necessary, for this reason, to appeal to relative local homotopy invariants
\[ \pi_i^Y(X, f)=\pi_i^Y(X, x)=\varprojlim_U \pi_{i-1}(U-U\cap Y, f(t))\qquad\text{for }i\geqslant 2, \]

(and an ad-hoc definition as above for \(i=0, 1\)), where \(Y\) is a closed part of \(X\); or to make up for the absence of general points by expressing the hypotheses on \(X\) in terms of properties of a topological nature (for the étale topology) of the spectra of the local rings of \(X\), which makes it possible to recover general points. The same reservation applies to the generalization of conjectures 4.2 and 4.3 to the case where \(X-Y\) is not assumed to be locally a complete intersection, a generalization suggested in the statement of the conditions b) of these conjectures.
In order to formulate the expurgated versions of conjectures 4.2 and 4.3 suggested by the results alluded to above, it is appropriate to lay down the

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1

Let \(X\) be a topological space, \(Y\) a locally closed part of \(X\), and \(n\) an integer. One says that \(X\) is of homotopy depth \(\geqslant n\) along \(Y\), and one writes \(\mathrm{prof\:hpt}_Y(X)\geqslant n\), if for every \(x\in Y\), one has \(\pi_i^Y(X, x)=0\) for \(i<n\).
It ought to be equivalent to say that for every open set \(X'\) of \(X\), and every \(x\in X'\cap U=U'\) (where \(U=X-Y\)), the canonical homomorphism
\[ \pi_i(U', x)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_i(X', x) \]
is an isomorphism for \(i<n-1\), a monomorphism for \(i=n-1\).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2

Let \(X\) be a complex analytic space, \(n\) an integer; one says that the rectified homotopy depth of \(X\) is \(n\)9, if for every locally closed analytic part \(Y\) of \(X\), one has

\[ \tag{x} {\mathrm{prof\:htp}_Y(X)\geqslant n-\dim Y} \]

(where, of course, \(\dim Y\) denotes the complex dimension of \(Y\)).

It ought to be equivalent to say that for every irreducible analytic part \(Y\) locally closed in \(X\), there exists a closed analytic part \(Z\) of \(Y\), of dimension \(<\dim Y\), such that the relation (x) holds for \(Y-Z\) instead of \(Y\). This would make it possible for example in definition 2 to restrict to the case where \(Y\) is non-singular.
The following conjecture, of a purely topological nature, is in the nature of a "local Hurewicz theorem".

blocks.label: A
blocks.title: Conjecture A
blocks.html:
Conjecture A

["local Hurewicz th."] Let \(X\) be a topological space, \(Y\) a locally closed part, subject if need be to conditions of "smoothness" of the kind of local triangulability of the pair \((X, Y)\), \(n\) an integer \(\geqslant 3\). In order to have \(\mathrm{prof\:htp}_Y(X)\geqslant n\), it is necessary and sufficient to have

\[ \operatorname{H}^i_Y(\mathbf{Z}_X)=0\quad\text{for } i<n \]

(one then says that \(X\) is of cohomological depth \(\geqslant n\) along \(Y\)), and that the local fundamental groups

\[ \pi^2_Y(X, x)=\varprojlim_{U\ni x} \pi_1(U-U\cap Y) \]

be zero (one then says that \(X\) is "pure" along \(Y\)).

One will note that if \(X\) is an analytic space, \(Y\) an analytic subspace, and if \(X\) is pure along \(Y\), then for every \(x\in Y\), the local ring \(\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\), as well as its localizations with respect to prime ideals containing the ideal defining the germ \(Y\) at \(x\) (i.e. lying in the inverse image \(Y_x\) of \(Y\) under \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_{X,x})=X_x\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\)) are pure in the sense of Exp. X; it seems plausible that the converse is also true. Analogous remarks hold for the cohomological depth, it being understood that one works with the étale topology on the \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_{X,x})\).
Conjecture 4.1 then generalizes into the

blocks.label: B
blocks.title: Conjecture B
blocks.html:
Conjecture B

["Purity"] Let \(E\) be an analytic space, \(X\) an analytic part of \(E\). Assume that \(E\) is non-singular of dimension \(N\) at \(x\), and that \(X\) can be described by \(p\) analytic equations in a neighbourhood of every point. Then the rectified homotopy depth of \(X\) is \(\geqslant N-p\).
In particular, a local complete intersection of dimension \(n\) at every point would be of rectified homotopy depth \(\geqslant n\), which is none other than conjecture 4.1.
Conjectures 4.2 and 4.3 generalize respectively into:

blocks.label: C
blocks.title: Conjecture C
blocks.html:
Conjecture C

["local Lefschetz"] Let \(X\) be an analytic space, \(Y\) a closed analytic part, \(x\) a point of \(Y\); assume that \(X-Y\) is Stein in a neighbourhood of \(x\) (for example \(Y\) defined by one equation at \(x\)), and that \(X-Y\) is of rectified homotopy depth \(\geqslant n\) in a neighbourhood of \(x\) (for example, is at every point of \(X-Y\) near \(x\) a complete intersection of dimension \(\geqslant n\), cf. conjecture B). Then the canonical homomorphism

\[ \pi_i^x(Y)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_i^x(X) \]

is an isomorphism for \(i<n-1\), an epimorphism for \(i=n-1\).

blocks.label: D
blocks.title: Conjecture D
blocks.html:
Conjecture D

["global Lefschetz"]

Let \(X\) be a compact analytic space, \(Y\) an analytic subspace of \(X\) such that \(U=X-Y\) is Stein, and is of rectified homotopy depth \(\geqslant n\) (for example a complete intersection of dimension \(\geqslant n\) at every point). Then the canonical homomorphism

\[ \pi_i(Y)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_i(X) \]

is an isomorphism for \(i<n-1\), an epimorphism for \(i=n-1\).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark

When, in statements C and D, one replaces the hypothesis that \(X-Y\) is Stein by the hypothesis that \(X-Y\) is a union of \(c+1\) Stein open sets (which will play the role of a topological "concavity" hypothesis), the conclusions must be modified simply by replacing therein \(n\) by \(n-c\).
Let us make explicit to finish, in the "global case" D, the conjecture concerning the fundamental group (obtained by taking \(n=3\)):

blocks.label: E
blocks.title: Conjecture D\(\boldsymbol'\)
blocks.html:
Conjecture D\(\boldsymbol'\)

[global Lefschetz for fundamental group]

Let \(X\) be a compact analytic space over the field of complex numbers, \(Y\) a closed analytic part such that \(U=X-Y\) is Stein. Assume moreover that the following conditions are satisfied:

  1. For every \(x\in U\), the local fundamental group \(\pi_2^x(X, x)\) is zero (i.e. \(X\) is "pure at \(x\)"), or merely the local ring \(\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\) is pure.

  2. The local rings of the points of \(U\) are "connected in dimension \(\geqslant 2\)".

  3. The local rings of the points of \(U\) are of dimension \(\geqslant 3\).

Under these conditions, for every \(x\in Y\), the homomorphism

\[ \pi_1(Y, x)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1(X, x) \]

is an isomorphism (and \(\pi_2(Y, x)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_2(X, x)\) an epimorphism).

One will note that the local conditions (i) (ii) (iii) on \(U\) are satisfied if \(U\) is locally a complete intersection of dimension \(\geqslant 3\). From the point of view of Algebraic Geometry (when \(U\) comes from a scheme, still denoted \(U\)), conditions (i) to (iii) correspond to hypotheses on the local invariants \(\pi_i^x(U)\), namely \(\pi_i^x(U)=0\) for \(i<3-\operatorname{deg\,tr} k(x)/k\), for the points \(x\) such that one has respectively \(\operatorname{deg\,tr} k(x)/k=0, 1, 2\). The global condition on \(U\) (\(U\) Stein) will be satisfied if \(X\) is projective and \(Y\) a hyperplane section.
